title: Working Family Payment (WFP) — Department of Social Protection
content:
Working Family Payment (WFP) is a weekly tax-free payment for employees with children. It is available to people who work at least 38 hours per fortnight (an average of 19 hours per week) and whose family income falls below certain limits.

Who can claim WFP
You qualify for WFP if:
- You are an employee (not self-employed) working at least 38 hours per fortnight
- You have at least one qualifying child living with you
- Your average weekly family income is below the income limit for your family size
- You are resident in Ireland

Children qualify if they are under 18, or under 22 and in full-time education.

Income limits (2025)
The income limits below are for average weekly gross family income (before tax):
- 1 child: €645
- 2 children: €746
- 3 children: €847
- 4 children: €938
- 5 children: €1,028
- 6 children: €1,124
- 7 children: €1,215
- 8+ children: €1,311

How much you receive
WFP is 60% of the difference between your average weekly family income and the income limit for your family size.

Example: Family with 2 children, average weekly income of €500.
Income limit for 2 children: €746. Difference: €246. WFP: 60% of €246 = €147.60 per week.

How to apply
Apply online through MyWelfare.ie or in person at your local Intreo office. You will need:
- Proof of employment (payslips for the last 6 weeks)
- Child's birth certificate
- Proof of address
- PPSN for you, your partner, and all children
- Your employer's contact details for verification

Payment is made directly to your bank account weekly. WFP lasts for 52 weeks. After 52 weeks, you must reapply and will be reassessed.

Used in conjunction with other payments
You can receive WFP alongside Back to Work Family Dividend, One-Parent Family Payment (OPFP), or Supplementary Welfare Allowance. However, you cannot receive WFP and Jobseeker's Allowance at the same time.

title: Carer's Allowance — Department of Social Protection
content:
Carer's Allowance is a means-tested payment for people who are providing full-time care and attention to a person who needs it because of age, illness, or disability.

Who can claim Carer's Allowance
You may qualify if:
- You are 18 or over
- You live with or near the person you are caring for
- You provide full-time care and attention to a person who requires it
- The person you care for is so incapacitated as to require full-time care
- You are not engaged in employment, self-employment, or training for more than 18.5 hours per week
- You satisfy the means test
- You are habitually resident in Ireland

Full-time care means the person being cared for needs continuous supervision and frequent assistance throughout the day — and possibly at night.

Rates 2025
- Under 66: €248 per week
- Age 66 and over: €282 per week

If you are caring for two people at the same time (for example, an elderly parent and an adult with a disability), you can receive 50% extra (half-rate Carer's Allowance in addition to your standard rate).

Means test
Carer's Allowance is means tested. Your weekly income from all sources (excluding the income of the person you are caring for, and certain other items) must fall below limits set by DSP. Capital (savings, property other than your home) is also assessed.

Carer's Support Grant
All Carer's Allowance recipients automatically receive the Carer's Support Grant — an annual payment of €1,850 (2025). It is paid in June each year. The grant is also available to some carers who are not receiving Carer's Allowance.

Half-rate Carer's Allowance
If you are already receiving another qualifying social welfare payment (such as a Widow's/Widower's Pension, Disability Allowance, or One-Parent Family Payment), you can receive half-rate Carer's Allowance in addition to your main payment.

How to apply
Apply by completing form CR1 (available at your Intreo office or on gov.ie) and submitting it to your local Intreo office. You will need medical evidence about the person being cared for. Processing time is typically 6-8 weeks.

Carer's Benefit
If you are employed and leave work to care for someone, you may qualify for Carer's Benefit (a PRSI-based payment) instead of Carer's Allowance. Carer's Benefit lasts up to 2 years and is not means tested.

title: Disability Allowance — Department of Social Protection
content:
Disability Allowance is a weekly means-tested payment for people aged 16 to 66 with a disability or illness that has substantially restricted their capacity to work for at least one year.

Who can claim Disability Allowance
You may qualify if:
- You are aged 16 to 66 (after 66, you may transfer to State Pension)
- You have a physical, sensory, mental health, or intellectual disability that substantially restricts your ability to work
- Your disability is expected to last at least one year
- You satisfy the means test
- You are habitually resident in Ireland
- You are not in full-time employment (though you can do rehabilitation or therapeutic work)

Rate 2025
€232 per week (personal rate)

Increases for dependants
- Qualified Adult (partner/spouse): €154 per week extra
- Each qualified child: €50 per week per child extra

Means test
The means test assesses your income from all sources — employment income, self-employment income, savings, investments, rental income. Your own income is assessed, as well as the income of your spouse/civil partner if applicable. Capital is also assessed (€20,000 is disregarded).

Work and Disability Allowance
You can work while receiving Disability Allowance under Partial Capacity Benefit or as part of a rehabilitative scheme:
- First €165 per week from employment is disregarded for means purposes
- Half of income between €165 and €375 per week is assessed
- All income above €375 is assessed

Medical assessment
You must provide medical evidence — typically a form completed by your GP or specialist — showing the nature of your condition and how it affects your ability to work. DSP's Medical Assessors may assess your case.

How to apply
Apply online at MyWelfare.ie or complete form DA1 available at your local Intreo office. Include medical evidence and details of your means. Processing time is typically 6-10 weeks.

Related payments
- Invalidity Pension: PRSI-based payment for people who cannot work due to illness (no means test)
- Blind Pension: for people with vision impairment
- Partial Capacity Benefit: for people returning to work from Illness Benefit or Invalidity Pension

title: Back to Education Allowance (BTEA) — Department of Social Protection
content:
Back to Education Allowance (BTEA) is a scheme that allows people receiving certain social welfare payments to return to full-time education while continuing to receive financial support.

Who can apply
You may qualify for BTEA if you are receiving one of the following for a minimum qualifying period:
- Jobseeker's Allowance or Jobseeker's Benefit (9 months on one payment or a combination)
- One-Parent Family Payment (any duration)
- Disability Allowance or Invalidity Pension (any duration)
- Blind Pension (any duration)
- Carer's Allowance (any duration, if you are no longer caring)
- Back to Education Allowance is also available to people leaving prison

Applicants aged 21 or over can access the scheme. However, people aged 18-21 on Jobseeker's must have been on the payment for 6 months.

Eligible courses
You can attend:
- Second-level courses (Junior Certificate, Leaving Certificate, PLC/Fetac Level 5 or 6)
- Third-level courses (degree, diploma, certificate at a recognised institution)
The course must be full-time and lead to a recognised qualification. Part-time courses are generally not eligible.

Rate of payment
You continue to receive your current welfare payment rate. You do not receive additional payment on top — BTEA is about keeping your payment while in education, not an extra payment.

Additional support
- An annual Cost of Education Allowance of €500 is payable to eligible BTEA recipients to help with education costs
- You may also be eligible for SUSI grants (see SUSI.ie) for maintenance and fee support

Effect on other payments
While receiving BTEA, your child's school meals and Back to School Clothing and Footwear Allowance continue as normal. However, some other supplements may cease.

How to apply
Apply before starting your course — late applications are generally not accepted. Contact your local Intreo office with:
- Proof you are currently on a qualifying payment
- Details of the course you plan to attend (name, institution, start date)
- A letter of acceptance from the college (or a conditional offer)

Back to Education Programme
Separate from BTEA, DSP offers a Back to Education Programme for people who want to take part-time courses or single modules while on a welfare payment. Check with your Intreo case officer for eligibility.

title: State Pension (Contributory) — Department of Social Protection
content:
The State Pension (Contributory) is a weekly payment from the Department of Social Protection, paid to people from the age of 66 who have sufficient PRSI (Pay Related Social Insurance) contributions.

Pension age
You can claim the State Pension (Contributory) from age 66. You do not have to retire to claim it — you can continue working and receive the pension.

PRSI requirements
To qualify for the full pension, you need:
- A minimum of 520 paid PRSI contributions (10 years of contributions)
- An average of at least 48 contributions per year over your working life (for the full rate)

A reduced pension is payable with fewer contributions. The minimum qualifying average is 10 contributions per year.

Total Contributions Approach (TCA)
Since 2019, DSP uses the Total Contributions Approach to calculate pensions. Under TCA, 2,080 paid or credited contributions give you the full pension. If you have fewer, your pension is proportionally reduced.

HomeCaring periods
If you spent time out of the workforce caring for children under 12 or a person with a disability, you may receive HomeCaring Period credits. Up to 20 years of HomeCaring Periods can be counted under TCA, helping carers (mainly women) who had gaps in employment.

Rate 2025
- Full rate (2,080+ contributions): €289.30 per week
- Proportional rates available for people with between 520 and 2,080 contributions

Qualified Adult
If you have a dependent spouse or civil partner, you can claim an Increase for a Qualified Adult (IQA) — up to €192.70 per week (2025), subject to the qualified adult's own income.

Living alone increase
If you live alone, you receive an additional €22 per week.

How to apply
Apply 3-6 months before your 66th birthday to avoid delays. Apply online at MyWelfare.ie, or get form SPC1 from your local Intreo office. You will need your PPSN and details of all employment (Irish and foreign PRSI may be combined under EU agreements).

Foreign PRSI contributions
If you worked in another EU/EEA country or a country with a bilateral social security agreement with Ireland, those contributions may count toward your Irish pension entitlement. Notify DSP when applying.

State Pension (Non-Contributory)
If you do not have enough PRSI contributions, you may qualify for the means-tested State Pension (Non-Contributory) — currently €254 per week (2025). It is available to people aged 66 who pass a means test.

title: One-Parent Family Payment (OFP) — Department of Social Protection
content:
One-Parent Family Payment (OFP) is a means-tested payment for men and women who are bringing up a child without the support of a partner. You must be the main carer for at least one child who is under 7 years of age.

Eligibility
You may qualify for OFP if:
- You are a lone parent (separated, divorced, widowed, unmarried, or have a partner in prison)
- You are the main carer for at least one child under 7
- Your average weekly earnings (after deductions) are below €425 per week
- You satisfy the habitual residence condition

Age limit for the youngest child
OFP is payable until the youngest qualifying child reaches age 7. At that point, you may be eligible to transfer to Jobseeker's Transitional Payment.

Rate 2025
€232 per week (personal rate)

Increase for a qualified child
- €50 per week for each qualified child

Means test
Your income from employment and other sources is assessed. The assessment includes:
- Employment income: the first €165 per week from employment is disregarded; 50% of income between €165 and €425 is assessed; income above €425 disqualifies you entirely
- Maintenance payments: assessed at up to a specific amount
- Capital and savings: assessed over a certain threshold

Working while receiving OFP
You can work while receiving OFP, subject to the earnings limits above. There is no restriction on hours worked, but income above the thresholds reduces or eliminates your payment.

Jobseeker's Transitional Payment
When your youngest child turns 7, you may transfer to the Jobseeker's Transitional Payment, which supports lone parents to move into employment. It is available until the youngest child turns 14.

How to apply
Apply using form OFP1, available from your local Intreo office or on gov.ie. Submit with:
- Child's birth certificate
- Proof of lone parent status (where applicable)
- Proof of income
- PPSN for you and each child

title: Supplementary Welfare Allowance (SWA) — Department of Social Protection
content:
Supplementary Welfare Allowance (SWA) is a means-tested payment administered by the Department of Social Protection through Intreo offices. It is designed to provide income support to people who do not qualify for any other social welfare payment, or who need urgent financial assistance while waiting for another payment to be processed.

Who can claim SWA
You may be eligible for SWA if:
- You have no income or very low income
- You are habitually resident in Ireland
- You satisfy the means test
- You are not in full-time education

SWA can also be paid on an urgent or exceptional basis to people who have an immediate need — even if they receive another payment — where that other payment is insufficient to meet basic needs.

Rate 2025
- Single person (18-24): €141 per week
- Single person (25+): €232 per week
- Couple (each 18+): €368 per week combined

Additional payments under SWA
As well as the basic payment, the following supplements can be paid under the SWA scheme:
- Rent Supplement: a contribution to rent costs for certain long-term renters (being phased out in favour of HAP)
- Mortgage Interest Supplement: for homeowners experiencing temporary difficulties (closed to new claimants)
- Back to School Clothing and Footwear Allowance
- Urgent Needs Payment: a single once-off payment for people in immediate financial crisis
- Diet Supplement: for people with a special dietary requirement due to a medical condition

Waiting for another payment
If you have applied for another social welfare payment (such as Jobseeker's Allowance, Disability Allowance, or One-Parent Family Payment) and it has not been processed yet, you can receive SWA as a bridging payment until your main payment starts.

How to apply
Contact your local Intreo office directly — do not apply online. Bring:
- PPSN
- Proof of identity and address
- Proof of income (or lack of income)
- Proof of residency
- Bank account details

SWA is administered locally — contact your nearest Intreo office to discuss your situation. Urgent Needs Payments can often be processed on the day.

title: Back to Work Enterprise Allowance (BTWEA) — Department of Social Protection
content:
Back to Work Enterprise Allowance (BTWEA) supports people who are long-term unemployed or on certain other DSP payments to start and develop their own business while retaining a portion of their social welfare payment.

Who can apply
You may qualify for BTWEA if you are receiving one of the following for the required period:
- Jobseeker's Allowance for at least 12 months (continuous or combined)
- Jobseeker's Benefit for at least 12 months
- One-Parent Family Payment for at least 12 months
- Disability Allowance for at least 12 months
- Blind Pension, Widow's/Widower's Pension, or certain other payments for at least 12 months
- You are leaving prison

The 12-month qualifying period does not need to be continuous — breaks in payments of up to 30 days can be bridged.

How much you receive
Under BTWEA, you keep a percentage of your current social welfare payment:
- Year 1: 100% of your qualifying payment
- Year 2: 75% of your qualifying payment

After 2 years, you are expected to be self-supporting from your business income.

Business support
As part of BTWEA, you are also eligible for business support from your Local Enterprise Office (LEO) — including mentoring, training, and in some cases financial grants. Contact your county's Local Enterprise Office for details.

Secondary Benefits
While on BTWEA, you retain your secondary benefits (Medical Card, Back to School Clothing and Footwear Allowance, etc.) for the duration of the scheme.

Self-Employment while on BTWEA
You must be genuinely self-employed — working in your own business. You cannot do casual work for a former employer under a BTWEA scheme.

Short-term Enterprise Allowance (STEA)
If you do not meet the 12-month qualifying period for BTWEA, you may qualify for the Short-term Enterprise Allowance. STEA pays at your current Jobseeker's Benefit rate for the remaining duration of your Jobseeker's Benefit entitlement.

How to apply
Contact your local Intreo office before starting your business — you must apply before beginning self-employment. Your case officer will assess your business plan and eligibility. DSP and your LEO work together to provide the full support package.
